trajectory_summary: The call opened with genuine customer energy — James was warm, engaged, and volunteered two strong pain points (roof cost, high bill) plus enthusiasm signals (pool pump, self-sufficiency, clean energy interest) within the first two minutes. Tyke built early credibility with a real-time satellite read of the property and a plausible technical reframe of the neighbor's bad experience. The call then stalled on a net metering uncertainty Tyke surfaced but could not resolve, ending in a soft callback with no appointment and no locked time. The energy James brought into the call was not converted.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the outcome is a soft callback — Tyke said 'I'll give you a callback' with no locked time, no appointment, and no confirmed next step. This is a low-probability follow-up, not a booked sit. The call should be tagged 'no_appointment / soft_callback.'

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Unconfirmed DQ — soft exit on a live lead
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I was gonna say, it looks like we might not be approved with Edisto. Let me double check here. They might not have a great net metering program, which would be the thing that would kind of, save you some money. Let me check with my design team. I don't wanna get too deep in the weeds for it yet. It looks like we're not gonna be able to work with them, and let me see if there's anything we could do.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Tyke exits a warm, engaged lead on an unconfirmed disqualifier. He surfaces the net metering uncertainty, explains the mechanism (scope creep), and then closes the call with a soft callback rather than escalating in real time. James had $250/month bill pain, a roof need, a Powerwall use case, and genuine enthusiasm — none of which were converted. The manager needs to establish the protocol: uncertain utility eligibility escalates to a supervisor or design team on the call, not after. The roofing sale was available regardless of solar eligibility and was never routed.

key_moments.quote: And what got you interested in looking at solar?
key_moments.stewart_read: Tyke opens the verify section with the codex's highest-value question immediately after confirming the address. The open-ended framing — 'what got you interested' rather than 'are you interested' — is exactly what the codex calls for. It directly unlocked James's 90-second disclosure covering bill pain, roof need, neighbor comparison, pool pump experimentation, and self-sufficiency interest. This is a positive verify execution worth crediting.

key_moments.quote: it's hard to get ahead in life already. I don't need somebody making money on me and then me having to pay another $100 a month because that won't happen. I'll just default because I can't I can't make I can't afford that.
key_moments.stewart_read: James discloses financial vulnerability — 'I'll just default' is not a rhetorical flourish, it's a real fear about affordability. Tyke's response ('so No. And that yeah. Those are all good concerns to have') is a generic acknowledgment that skips past the emotional weight entirely. The codex classifies this as a trust gift: the customer is telling you exactly what they're afraid of. The correct move is to acknowledge specifically before any technical reframe.

key_moments.quote: his electric bill went down, and he also kinda got a a roof restoration or repair out of it, or new roof. And my wife and I are looking at having to spend money on a roof right now
key_moments.stewart_read: James volunteers two facts in the same breath: his friend got a roof through solar, and he and his wife are facing roof costs right now. This is a live cross-sell signal — Ion does roofing in-house, both standalone and as solar-install prep. Tyke does not bridge to Ion's roofing capability here. He later says at [03:08] 'I'm more than happy to kinda take a look at that with our every roof option as well' — a partial acknowledgment — but it arrives after the roof topic has already passed and is hedged ('kinda,' 'every roof option' appears to be a transcription artifact). The codex template calls for an active bridge: 'Ion actually does roofing in-house — we could potentially do both for you.'

key_moments.quote: I'm more than happy to kinda take a look at that with our every roof option as well.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is Tyke's second pass at the roofing signal — a partial attempt that falls short of the codex template. The hedged phrasing ('kinda,' 'every roof option') and passive framing ('take a look at that') do not constitute a bridge to Ion's roofing team. The codex calls for naming the capability explicitly and offering to route: 'Ion does roofing in-house — want me to have our roofing team take a look?' Tyke's line acknowledges the topic exists but does not move it forward.
